retrieved_docs.title: Long Arm Splinting -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Preparation of a long arm cast involves several steps. The practitioner may decide on the width of plaster or fiberglass to use that best fits the patient's anatomy. For adults, this often translates to 3-inch diameter sugar tong and 3 to 4-inch posterior mold material.  For pediatrics, 2-inch sugar tong and posterior mold often work well. For plaster and fiberglass, the length of the sugar tong and posterior splint should be pre-measured. With plaster, eight to 10 layers are often used. For fiberglass, most splinting materials come pre-fabricated. Synthetic or cotton web roll should be obtained for padding. Optionally, stockinette can be used for patient comfort as a base layer before splinting. The final overwrap is typically performed with an elastic bandage.

One can also add multiple bars to the same pins to enhance stability. Bars get secured to the pins by clamps. The most common material for bars today is carbon fiber, which is 15 % stiffer than stainless steel bars. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Long Arm Splinting -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Long arm splints are a valuable tool in the treatment of a variety of upper extremity injuries. This form of splinting can provide excellent immobilization while allowing for swelling that often accompanies acute injuries. Typically, a long arm splint is the initial form of immobilization. It typically is removed, and patients are transitioned into more definitive immobilization, such as a cast. The practitioner needs to take time to become familiar with the materials and techniques needed to make a well-padded and molded splint that properly positions a patient to maintain a reduction and allow for soft tissue swelling.

retrieved_docs.title: Volar Splinting -- Technique or Treatment -- Splinting Technique
retrieved_docs.content: The side of the extremity contralateral to the fracture should be measured to calculate the necessary length of the casting material. When creating a plaster splint, fold the plaster into layers and dip it into the container of water at an angle of 45 degrees. Completely immerse the plaster. Once the bubbling ceases, the plaster may be removed from the water. Squeeze out the excess water using longitudinal compression massage. If the splinting material is too long, the edges may be folded over to achieve the desired length. For an average-sized adult, an upper extremity splint will require approximately 10 layers of casting material. [28] The splint will generate more heat and weigh more if excess sheets are used. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Forearm Splinting -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Apply appropriate mold while the plaster is hardening.

retrieved_docs.title: Long Arm Splinting -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: To efficiently assemble a long arm cast, the practitioner should recruit the help of at least one other person. Have an assistant hold the patient's extremity in the desired position of immobilization while the splint is being created and molded. This can help with the accurate placement of materials and minimize impingement of structures that could otherwise need to be adjusted after splint application (e.g., anterior cubital fossa).

retrieved_docs.title: Splinting -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Splinting is not a benign procedure; improper splint application may result in adverse outcomes. One study found that up to 40% of patients splinted in the emergency department developed soft tissue complications, including skin ulceration in 6% of patients. [5] There is still debate whether casting or splinting has a lower complication rate in the immediate postoperative management of pediatric supracondylar fractures. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Wrist Splint -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to recognize the complications that may arise not only from performing wrist splinting but those which may occur due to the underlying injury as well. Compartment syndrome is a major complication resulting from increased pressure within fascial planes, which leads to impaired venous and lymphatic flow, which may be due to increased internal swelling or an excessively tight cast/splint. This is an emergency, and it is, therefore, crucial that patients be educated regarding the six signs and symptoms of compartment syndrome – pain out of proportion (early), paresthesia, poikilothermia, paresis, pallor, and pulselessness (late). If caused by internal pressure, treatment is an emergent surgical fasciotomy, whereas increased pressure due to a cast/splint is relieved by cutting the cast or loosening splint or elastic wrap. Improper positioning, splint fabrication, or lack of padding can cause pressure ulcers, lacerations, increased pain/swelling, and tendon rupture. [8] Improper alignment of a fracture may lead to poor healing and poor functional outcome and may require corrective surgery. Finally, excessively pressure exhibited onto the volar distal forearm can cause acute carpal tunnel syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Splinting -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Patient-specific anatomy must be accounted for when fashioning a splint. The splint should be fashioned to restore the anatomic resting joint position, minimizing adverse outcomes. The mainstays of acute immobilization are plaster or fiberglass splints. Prefabricated splints, such as foam splints or braces, may play a role in the immobilization of chronic injuries to provide structural support or pain control but are less commonly used in the acute fracture setting. Plaster is the preferred malleable splinting material to maintain a position-specific reduction, but it is limited by drying time, user experience, and provider-placed mold. Fiberglass splints are lighter, easier to apply, and more porous but are more expensive and provide a less-reliable mold.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

Together, these entities define an integrated framework of thigh‑and‑leg orthopedic care that spans growth modulation, fracture fixation, malunion correction, lengthening/shortening, prosthetic revision, soft‑tissue repair, and preventive strategies, all contextualized within the broader impact of osteoporosis on lower‑extremity bone health.

Internal‑fixation procedures (identified as “تثبيت داخلي للكسر”) are associated with multiple treatment categories: “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur,” “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur),” and “fracture or dislocation of radius and ulna.” These associations emphasize that bone quality influences surgical decision‑making for both hip and peripheral limb injuries. Within the osteoporosis SMQ, additional members such as “جراحة مفصل الورك” (hip joint surgery), “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (hip head prosthesis), and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 755
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” is a subcategory of “treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur)” and encompasses a range of operative interventions focused on the femur, proximal tibia, fibula, and trochanteric region. Specific procedures classified as children of this category include “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” which addresses growth‑plate modulation of the distal femur, and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus),” which corrects angular deformities of the lower limb by partially arresting growth. The category also contains “Internal fixation of femur” and its variant “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” both of which provide stable fixation for femoral fractures, with the latter used when anatomical reduction is not achieved. Bone‑shaping and lengthening techniques are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” and the more complex “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” which allow for correction of limb length discrepancies and deformities through controlled osteotomy and segment manipulation. Management of post‑traumatic deformity is addressed by “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” which restores proper alignment of the femur distal to the proximal femoral region. Additionally, “Trochanterplasty” is listed as a child procedure, indicating surgical modification of the greater trochanter for indications such as hip abductor insufficiency or prosthetic fitting. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the comprehensive surgical toolkit available for addressing fractures, dislocations, growth‑plate disorders, angular deformities, malunions, and length discrepancies in the thigh and leg, thereby supporting optimal functional recovery and limb alignment.
